FairyWinx's blog

Codeforces Round 1021 Editorial
By FairyWinx, history, 11 months ago, translation,
Div2A

2098A - Vadim's Collection

Our goal is to obtain the minimally possible string that satisfies the conditions of beauty. Therefore, we need to arrange the digits in order from the first to the last, each time choosing the minimally possible suitable digit.

More formally:

  • For the ii-th position, we need to place the smallest available digit that is not less than 10−i10−i.
  • After placing a digit, it becomes unavailable for further use.
  • We repeat the process for all 1010 positions.

Why does this work? Notice that when we place a digit in the ii-th position, we have at least ii digits in the original number that are greater than or equal to 10−i10−i, meaning that at most i−1i−1 digits have been used from them earlier, and there is always some option available. Thus, at each step, it is beneficial to choose the smallest available suitable digit because the number can always be completed to the end, and choosing a larger digit would result in a larger number.

Div2B

2098B - Sasha and the Apartment Purchase

Note that for a fixed position, the optimal point is the median (or for an even number of elements, any point between the two medians).

Proof:

Observe that if we move the point between xx and x+1x+1, the answer changes by the difference in the number of elements to the left and right between xx and x+1x+1. Therefore, f(x)f(x) monotonically decreases to the median. This means that the optimum is indeed located at the median.

Now the problem has been reduced to the following: how many such points exist such that if we remove no more than kk of the original elements, this point remains the median. Note that to obtain the optimal answer, we can simply remove elements from the beginning or from the end, since if two elements are on different sides of the median, this action does nothing. Thus, our answer lies within the segment, and it is (the formulas need to be adjusted with the correct plus or minus one, we will leave this as an exercise for the participants) an+k2−an−k2an+k2−an−k2, where aa is sorted beforehand.

Div1A

2097A - Sports Betting

Let's sort the array aa in non-decreasing order: a1≤a2≤…≤ana1≤a2≤…≤an. Suppose Vadim argues with the students in turn. We can formalize the problem as follows: since there are only two possible seating arrangements each day, we can encode them with the digits 00 and 11. Each of Vadim's predictions can be represented by a pair of numbers (ci,di)(ci,di)—the seating arrangement for days ai+1ai+1 and ai+2ai+2, respectively.

Vadim will be able to win in the following cases:

  • If there exists a quadruple of students ai=ai+1=ai+2=ai+3ai=ai+1=ai+2=ai+3, meaning those students he will argue with on the same day. In this case, he can provide all possible 44 predictions for days ai+1ai+1 and ai+2ai+2 and will guarantee at least one correct prediction.
  • If there exists a pair of indices i<ji<j such that ai=ai+1<aj=aj+1ai=ai+1<aj=aj+1 and for each x∈{ai+1,ai+2,…,aj−1}x∈{ai+1,ai+2,…,aj−1} there exists a kk such that ak=xak=x.
    • Suppose on day xx and day yy (x<yx<y) Vadim argues with at least two students, and on days x+1,…,y−1x+1,…,y−1 with at least one student.
    • On day xx, Vadim makes predictions (0,1)(0,1) and (1,1)(1,1).
    • On each of the days x+1,…,y−1x+1,…,y−1, Vadim makes predictions (0,1)(0,1).
    • On day yy, Vadim makes predictions (0,0)(0,0) and (0,1)(0,1).

To prove the correctness of Vadim's strategy, consider the string sx+1…sy+1sy+2sx+1…sy+1sy+2—the seating arrangements from day xx to day yy. If sx+1≠0sx+1≠0 or sy+1≠1sy+1≠1, then Vadim can definitively convince at least one student on day xx or day yy. Otherwise, if sx+1=0sx+1=0 and sy+1=1sy+1=1, then there exists a day zz (x≤z<yx≤z<y) such that sz+1=0sz+1=0 and sz+2=1sz+2=1, which means Vadim can convince a student on day zz.

It can be shown that in all other cases, Vadim loses. To demonstrate this, we need to fix an arbitrary strategy of Vadim's and try to find a counterexample to his strategy. The general plan for the proof is as follows:

  • If ai+2≤aiai+2≤ai, then the segments of students [1,i][1,i] and [i+1,n][i+1,n] can be considered independently. Let's consider a block of students with whom Vadim argues without breaks.
  • If a1<a2a1<a2 or an−1<anan−1<an, then the first or, respectively, the last student can be easily discarded without affecting the other students.
  • Otherwise, we have a1=a2≤…≤an−1=ana1=a2≤…≤an−1=an, which is the only case where Vadim has no winning strategy according to the previous point, when n≤3n≤3.
Div1B

2097B - Baggage Claim

If any pair of adjacent cells p2i−1p2i−1 and p2i+1p2i+1 is at a distance other than 22, then the answer is 00.

Otherwise, for each such pair of cells, there are two possible cases:

  • Cells p2i−1p2i−1 and p2i+1p2i+1 are in the same row or column. Then cell p2ip2i must be located between them.
  • In the other case, there are two possible positions for cell p2ip2i.

We will construct a graph where the vertices are all the cells of the field, and edges are created for each even cell p2,…,p2kp2,…,p2k. If cell p2ip2i can only be in position αα, we draw a self-loop at vertex αα. If cell p2ip2i can be in position αα or ββ, we draw an edge connecting these two vertices.

Thus, we need to choose an incident vertex for each edge such that each vertex is chosen at most once. It is clear that this problem can be solved independently for each connected component, and then we multiply the answers for all components:

  • If a component with ss vertices contains more than ss edges, then the answer is 00 (Dirichlet's principle).
  • If a component with ss vertices has exactly ss edges, then the component contains exactly one cycle.
    • If this cycle is a loop, then the answer is 11.
    • If it is a non-degenerate cycle, then the answer is 22.
  • If the component with ss vertices is a tree, then the answer is ss (we choose an unchosen vertex, and then everything is determined).
Div1C

2097C - Bermuda Triangle

We will use the idea of reflections. Instead of saying that the plane is reflected with respect to a side, we will say that it actually flew further but ended up in the triangle that results from reflecting the original triangle with respect to that side.

All such triangles form a pattern of the following kind. The vertices of the triangles are located at all possible points of the form (nx,ny)(nx,ny) (x,y∈Zx,y∈Z). Accordingly, it is easy to see that we can reduce the velocity vector such that gcd(vx,vy)=1gcd(vx,vy)=1. Then, to determine the time it will take for the plane to exit the triangle, we use the Chinese remainder theorem:

vxt+x≡0(modn)vxt+x≡0(modn)

vyt+y≡0(modn)vyt+y≡0(modn)

Accordingly, we can compute such a minimal non-negative suitable tt. Next, we need to calculate the number of reflections. Essentially, this is the number of times the segment (x,y)(x,y), (vxt+x,vyt+y)(vxt+x,vyt+y) intersects the lines in the figure above.

If the endpoint is (tx⋅n,ty⋅n)(tx⋅n,ty⋅n), then the number of intersections is as follows: the number of intersections with vertical segments is tx−1tx−1, with horizontal segments is ty−1ty−1, the number of lines parallel to the hypotenuse of the original triangle is ⌊12[tx+ty]⌋⌊12[tx+ty]⌋, and the number of lines perpendicular to the original hypotenuse is ⌊12|tx−ty|⌋⌊12|tx−ty|⌋.

Div1D

2097D - Homework

The problem itself is trivial, but solving it requires not skipping lectures on linear algebra. Suspecting that not all participants are familiar with the basics of linear algebra, we will try to explain each step in detail.

Part 1. Matrices.

Let n=k⋅mn=k⋅m, where mm is an odd number, and kk is a power of two. Any binary string ss of length nn can be represented as a matrix M(s)M(s):

M(s)=⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜s1sm+1⋮sn−m+1s2sm+2⋮sn−m+2⋯⋯⋱⋯sms2m⋮sn⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟M(s)=(s1s2⋯smsm+1sm+2⋯s2m⋮⋮⋱⋮sn−m+1sn−m+2⋯sn)

It is easy to prove that any operation μμ (one move, several moves, or none) that transforms string ss into string tt can be expressed as μ^⋅M(s)=M(t)μ^⋅M(s)=M(t), where μ^μ^ is a matrix in Zk×k2Z2k×k. Indeed:

  • If k=1k=1, the only possible operation μμ does nothing, and μ^=Inμ^=In (the identity matrix).
  • If the operation μμ adds the second half of ss to the first half, then μ^μ^ can be written in block form:
    μ^=(III0)μ^=(III0)
  • If the operation μμ adds the first half to the second half, then μ^μ^ can also be written in block form.
  • If μμ independently changes the left and right halves of ss using operations μ1μ1 and μ2μ2, then μ^μ^ has the form:
    μ^=(μ^100μ^2)μ^=(μ^100μ^2)
  • If μμ is a combination of operations μ1,…,μlμ1,…,μl, then μ^=μl^…μ1^μ^=μl^…μ1^.

Part 2. Torment and Suffering.

Proposition. For any invertible matrix A∈GL(Zk2)A∈GL(Z2k), there exists an operation μμ such that μ^=Aμ^=A.

Proof. The case k=1k=1 is trivial; for k=2k=2, we can explicitly provide expressions for elementary transformations. Adding one row to another is trivial, and swapping them can be done as follows:

(xy)→(x+yy)→(x+yx+2y)=(x+yx)→(yx)(xy)→(x+yy)→(x+yx+2y)=(x+yx)→(yx)

Comment. Yes, this is the implementation of std::swap using the XOR operation.

Now consider the case k>2k>2. By the induction hypothesis, we can apply any invertible transformation to the halves of string ss at any moment. Let x1,…,xtx1,…,xt be the first t=k2t=k2 rows of the matrix M(s)M(s), and y1,…,yty1,…,yt be the second half of the rows. We will show that we can add row y1y1 to row x1x1 using the specified operations:

⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1x2⋮xty1y2⋮yt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟→⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1+x2x1⋮xty1y2⋮yt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟→⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1+x2x1⋮xty1+x1+x2y2+x1⋮yt+xt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟→⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1+x2x1⋮xty1+y2+x2y2+x1⋮yt+xt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟→⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1+x2x1⋮xty1+y2+x1y2⋮yt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟→⎛⎝⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜⎜x1x2⋮xty1+x1y2⋮yt⎞⎠⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟⎟(x1x2⋮xty1y2⋮yt)→(x1+x2x1⋮xty1y2⋮yt)→(x1+x2x1⋮xty1+x1+x2y2+x1⋮yt+xt)→(x1+x2x1⋮xty1+y2+x2y2+x1⋮yt+xt)→(x1+x2x1⋮xty1+y2+x1y2⋮yt)→(x1x2⋮xty1+x1y2⋮yt)

Then, to add any row from the second half to the first, we should shuffle the rows in the first and second halves so that the required rows are at the top, perform the specified sequence of actions, and then restore the original order of the rows. Similarly, a row from the first half can be added to the second half.

Thus, any row can be added to any other. And since swapping two rows is realized through this operation, and considering that matrices live in Z2Z2, this means that any elementary operation can be represented in terms of the operations described. Since any invertible matrix AA can be represented as a composition of elementary operations, it can be represented as a composition of some operations μμ.

To summarize, this proof turned out to be a bit more unpleasant than we expected. A good concept here would be to feel the idea of this proof instead of getting bogged down in every technical transition.

Part 3. A Cute Algorithm.

To check whether string ss can be transformed into string tt, based on the above proof, it is sufficient to check whether there exists an invertible matrix AA such that A⋅M(s)=M(t)A⋅M(s)=M(t). We apply the Gaussian elimination algorithm to M(s)M(s) and M(t)M(t), which uses row operations to bring M(s)M(s) and M(t)M(t) to reduced row echelon form G(s)G(s) and G(t)G(t), respectively.

Then the answer to the problem is "Yes" if and only if G(s)=G(t)G(s)=G(t), which follows from the uniqueness of the reduced row echelon form of a matrix.

The Gaussian elimination algorithm for a matrix MM of size m×km×k runs in time:

O(mk⋅rnkB)=O(mk⋅min{m,k})=O(nn−−√)O(mk⋅rnkB)=O(mk⋅min{m,k})=O(nn)

Since we are working with bit matrices, using std::bitset, the algorithm can be improved to run in time O(nn√w)O(nnw); however, the solution without bitsets also runs in an acceptable time despite the large constraints in the problem.

Div1E

2097E - Clearing the Snowdrift

We will assign to each operation the maximum aiai on the segment of that operation. Consider some order of operations that zeroes out the entire array. If there are two adjacent operations in which the maximum on the segment of the earlier operation is smaller, then we can swap these two operations without changing anything (we leave this simple case analysis as an exercise for the reader). Thus, from any sequence, we can obtain a sequence of operations of the same length where the assigned maximums on the segments of the operations do not increase during the process.

Now let CC denote the maximum element in the array. Then one of the optimal sequences of operations will first decrease by one all numbers equal to CC, then all numbers equal to C−1C−1, and so on. How can we calculate the minimum number of operations needed to decrease all numbers equal to the current maximum in the array by one? We will look at the positions of these maximums (let's denote this set of positions as SS); they need to be covered by the minimum number of segments of length no more than dd. This is a standard problem that can be solved using a greedy algorithm:

  • Take the leftmost uncovered point x∈Sx∈S, place a segment [x,min(x+d−1,n)][x,min(x+d−1,n)],
  • Remove all points from S∩[x,min(x+d−1,n)]S∩[x,min(x+d−1,n)],
  • Repeat until SS is empty.

Now we can solve the problem in O(n⋅C)O(n⋅C), but that is slow. Let's denote the sorted set of numbers in the array as x1<x2…<xkx1<x2…<xk. We will also introduce x0=0x0=0. While we are decreasing the maximums from xixi to xi−1xi−1, the set SS does not change, meaning the greedy process is the same and always selects the same number of segments. Thus, it is sufficient to run the greedy process once. We obtain a solution in O(n2)O(n2).

Now let's optimize this solution. We only need to find the number of segments in the greedy process for the sets Sj={i:ai≥xj},0≤j≤kSj={i:ai≥xj},0≤j≤k. Let's simulate this process simultaneously from left to right. Consider a1a1. Let a1=xia1=xi. Then for all sets numbered from 00 to ii, a segment of length dd starts at this element. Let's remember this and say that these sets will return to consideration when we reach the processing of ad+1ad+1, because up to that point the elements are covered by the segment. We will continue doing this. We take all sets with numbers less than or equal to jj (where the current element of the array is equal to xjxj), for which the segments have already ended, update the answer, and say that these sets will finish their segment after dd processed elements in the scanline. These are some operations with sets that can be maintained in a segment tree, which essentially stores the indices of the sets included in the segment tree, meaning only the paths to the leaves corresponding to the numbers of these sets are stored (somewhat similar to an implicit segment tree). The asymptotic complexity of the solution is O(n⋅log(n))O(n⋅log⁡(n)). Similar operations can be maintained in a Cartesian tree, but that would lead to a larger constant and O(n⋅log2(n))O(n⋅log2⁡(n)), which should not pass the time limits. For more details, you can read the author's solution.

There is also a solution using link-cut trees, which is simpler to understand but requires knowledge of this structure, so it was not intended as the author's solution.

Div1F

2097F - Lost Luggage

It is clear that the problem can be solved using flows. We will introduce a dummy source ss and (m+1)n(m+1)n vertices (i,j)(i,j) (0≤i≤m0≤i≤m, 1≤j≤n1≤j≤n), and the following edges will be added to the network:

  • For all jj (1≤j≤n1≤j≤n), edges from ss to (0,j)(0,j) with capacity sjsj.
  • For all pairs ii, jj (1≤i≤m1≤i≤m, 1≤j≤n1≤j≤n), edges from (i−1,j)(i−1,j) to (i,(j−2)modn+1)(i,(j−2)modn+1) with capacity ai,jai,j.
  • For all pairs ii, jj (1≤i≤m1≤i≤m, 1≤j≤n1≤j≤n), edges from (i−1,j)(i−1,j) to (i,j)(i,j) with capacity bi,jbi,j.
  • For all pairs ii, jj (1≤i≤m1≤i≤m, 1≤j≤n1≤j≤n), edges from (i−1,j)(i−1,j) to (i,jmodn+1)(i,jmodn+1) with capacity ci,jci,j.

To each layer ii (1≤i≤m1≤i≤m), we will connect edges to the sink titi (for all jj, edges from (i,j)(i,j) to titi will have capacity ∞∞). Thus, the answer to the problem is the values of the maximum flows from ss to t1,t2,…,tmt1,t2,…,tm, respectively.

Unfortunately for the participants, the maximum flows f1,…,fmf1,…,fm (fifi is the maximum flow from ss to titi) can differ significantly from each other. This causes algorithms based on the Ford-Fulkerson method to work unjustifiably slowly, specifically in time Ω(m2n3)Ω(m2n3). If we are wrong, and you managed to squeeze your solution, please share it in the comments :)

The values of the maximum flows can be found using the Ford-Fulkerson theorem with the help of dynamic programming. Specifically, let dp[k][msk]dp[k][msk] be the minimum STST-cut on the subgraph s∪{(i,j)}k,ni,j=0,1s∪{(i,j)}i,j=0,1k,n, where the vertex (k,j)(k,j) is in the TT part of the cut if and only if the jj-th bit of the mask mskmsk is equal to 11.

This dynamic can be easily computed in time O(m⋅4n)O(m⋅4n), but again, this is too slow. The author's solution is to speed up the recalculation of the next layer of dynamics to time O(2n⋅n)O(2n⋅n).

Let prv=dp[i−1]prv=dp[i−1] be the already computed layer of dynamics, and nxt=dp[i]nxt=dp[i] be the layer of dynamics that we want to compute. Instead of indexing by the mask in the forms, we will index these arrays by sets of vertices of the corresponding layer that are in the TT part of the cut.

nxt[U]=minW{prv[W]+∑i=1n∑j=−11[i+j∉W]⋅[i∈U]⋅e(i,j)}nxt[U]=minW{prv[W]+∑i=1n∑j=−11[i+j∉W]⋅[i∈U]⋅e(i,j)}

A couple of clarifications:

  • e(i,j)e(i,j) is simply the weights of the edges between layers i−1i−1 and ii in a convenient order. It is very difficult for me to keep track of the indices accurately, and this is just a technical detail, so reconstruct how to form e(i,j)e(i,j) from the arrays aiai, bibi, and cici from the context.
  • i+j∉Wi+j∉W actually means (i+j−1)modn+1∉W(i+j−1)modn+1∉W.
  • Here [P][P] is the Iverson bracket. [P]=1[P]=1 when the predicate PP is satisfied, and [P]=0[P]=0 otherwise.

The main difficulty of the recalculation lies in the fact that the set UU determines which summands will participate in the sum and which will not. For a fixed UU, each zero bit in the mask WW is assigned its own penalty; in other words, we define λU(i)=∑1j=−1[i−j∈U]e(i,j)λU(i)=∑j=−11[i−j∈U]e(i,j), then:

nxt[U]=minW{prv[W]+∑i=1n[i∉W]⋅λU(i)}nxt[U]=minW{prv[W]+∑i=1n[i∉W]⋅λU(i)}

To efficiently recalculate, we will build a segment tree. Formally, it will be defined as follows:

  • The segment tree contains n+1n+1 layers numbered 0,1,2,…,n0,1,2,…,n.
  • The indices of the vertices of the ii-th layer are subsets {1,2,…,i}{1,2,…,i}, and the two children of the vertex LL of the ii-th layer are the vertices LL and L∪{i+1}L∪{i+1} from the (i+1)(i+1)-th layer.
  • The value at the vertex LL from the ii-th layer is equal to x(i,L)x(i,L), where:

    x(i,L)=minD⊆{i+1,…,n}{prv[L∪D]+∑j=i+1n[j∉W]⋅λU(j)}x(i,L)=minD⊆{i+1,…,n}{prv[L∪D]+∑j=i+1n[j∉W]⋅λU(j)}

It is clear that the value x(i−1,L)x(i−1,L) can be easily computed as x(i−1,L)=min{x(i,L)+λU(i),x(i,L∪{i}}x(i−1,L)=min{x(i,L)+λU(i),x(i,L∪{i}}. This structure is good because when changing UU, we only need to recalculate the constructed tree up to the last layer where the weight function λUλU changed.

Now we will iterate over UU in the order of Gray codes approximately in the following order:

∅→{2}→{2,3}→{3}→…{1,…}→…∅→{2}→{2,3}→{3}→…{1,…}→…

The essence of this process is that we will iterate through all possible sets UU, where the 2nd bit will change 2n−12n−1 times, the 3rd bit will change 2n−22n−2 times, and so on, the nn-th bit will change 22 times, while the 1st bit will change only once. When changing the ii-th bit in UU, the function λUλU will change at points i−1i−1, ii, and i+1i+1, thus:

  • If 2≤i≤n−12≤i≤n−1, only the first i+1i+1 layers of the segment tree will change, and it will take 2i+22i+2 operations to recalculate them, which will happen 2n+1−i2n+1−i times.
  • If i=1i=1 or i=ni=n, the entire tree will need to be rebuilt, but this will only happen 33 times.

The total number of affected vertices when iterating over UU will be:

3⋅2n+1+∑i=1n−12i+1⋅2n+1−i=3⋅2n+1+(n−2)2n+2=(2n−1)⋅2n+1=O(n⋅2n)3⋅2n+1+∑i=1n−12i+1⋅2n+1−i=3⋅2n+1+(n−2)2n+2=(2n−1)⋅2n+1=O(n⋅2n)

Here the constant is not very good and the time constraints are strict, so we will also have to put effort into the implementation. I think it is worth mentioning that using a segment tree on pointers is not a very good idea, and one should write the segment tree in the same indexing as a regular segment tree.

  • -703
  • FairyWinx
  • 11 months ago
  • 132

    • »
      »
      »
      adiroy2
      11 months ago, show (+6) #
      »
      »
      »
      adiroy2
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +8

      Yea sure man. (apologies, i dont know how to use the markdown thingy for symbol text)

      Think of it this way. there is a number line from 0 to 1e9. an array is given, of n numbers. a0, a1, a2... an. these numbers lie on the number line. anywhere. these are the houses.

      You are also given a number k. now thing is, at a point of time when you buy any house, at most "k" amount of houses could be in a state "already sold" so you cant buy them. So what can you buy? the n-k houses. The function will be Σ|x — y|, which is basically the distance between the ith house (you want to buy) and all the other houses.

      Which basically boils down to this:

      For the ith house, what is the minimum cost you can get, after removing at most k houses.

      Suppose you have houses at locations: [1, 2, 3, 10], and k = 1. So you are allowed to remove at most 1 house to minimize your total distance.

      Now, for example, if you pick house 2:

      Without removing anything: cost ==> |2-1| + |2-2| + |2-3| + |2-10| = 1 + 0 + 1 + 8 = 10

      You can remove at most 1 house. Obviously, the farthest house (10) is causing most of the trouble.

      Remove house 10 , which leads to ==> new cost = |2-1| + |2-2| + |2-3| = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2

      So after removal, cost becomes 2.

      Now for different houses, you will get different minimized costs after removing at most 1 house. The goal is to find which house/houses can achieve the minimum possible cost (after removal) and count them.

      The editorial talks about median :- why??

      coz u wanna minimize Σ|x — y|, so u should choose a median of the points (after removing the worst k).

      Kindly correct if i am wrong ( i think i might be, took me a lot of time to think this out -_-)

      → Reply

          11 months ago, show (+1) #

          adiroy2
          11 months ago, hide # ^ |
          ← Rev. 2 →  
          +1

          6 2

          5 1 9 10 13 2

          so this means we hvae n = 6 (6 bars or houses) k = 2 (up to 2 bars can "close", i.e., you can remove up to 2 bars)

          1. sort this to get ==> [1, 2, 5, 9, 10, 13]
          2. we want to reduce costs, so remove all k choices possible,and keep n-k = 4 choices open for buying

          3. SO now u need to see which segment to keep. u can remove 1 from left and 13 from right, OR you can remove 1 and 2 from left, or 10 and 13 from right, and so on and so forth.

      +7
      • The problem statement must be written so that it can be understood unambiguously without samples.
      • Notes to the samples actually promote misunderstandings -- they actually trick you into thinking that Sasha chooses the house first, then the set of closed bars are selected so that f(x)f(x) becomes minimum. The actual problem is the opposite -- first the closed bars are selected, and xx is chosen so that f(x)f(x) is minimum.

        https://oi.wiki/math/number-theory/crt/#%E6%89%A9%E5%B1%95%E6%A8%A1%E6%95%B0%E4%B8%8D%E4%BA%92%E8%B4%A8%E7%9A%84%E6%83%85%E5%86%B5 this is a website in chinese about extended CRT,you can translate it.

      • »
        »
        »
        »
        Maxu
        11 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        Maxu
        11 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        +3

        Seems this can be solved by extended Chinese Remainder Theorem(exCRT)

        (vx)t+x≡0(modn) (vy)t+y≡0(modn) I merged these 2 functions to 1 function: (vx)t+x — ((vy)t+y) ≡ 0 (modn) Then this can be solved by Extended Euclidean Algorithm(exGCD)

        → Reply

How to come up with Div1B ?

Drawing the graph on paper and it kinda makes sense but i'm not sure how to prove that if it has as many edges as vertices there is exactly one loop, that if it has less it is a tree and that if it's a tree there's #vertices possibilities.

Also i'm not sure what intuition leads you to studying that graph ? (I was trying to count perfect matchings in the bipartite graph between odd and even p's)

Does these kind of graph to solve interleaving constraints have a name ? Is somewhat of a general technique ?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    arman_ferdous
    7 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    arman_ferdous
    7 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You were on the right track with the number of perfect matchings in bipartite graph. The key detail here is that all nodes have degree <= 2, which lets us get the count too. The solution to this problem which I've seen before does the same thing as the editorial.

    If a node from the left side of bipartite graph connects to u and v at the right side, you create a new graph where there is an edge between u and v. Do this modification for all pairs of edges in the original graph (if there is only one edge, in other words only u, add the self loop u-u instead).

    The problem of perfect matchings in bipartite graph has now been converted to giving direction to the edges in the new graph. Now you can figure out the rest yourself.

    I learnt this idea from here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PdCnYQqadlQ (Around 15 mins is where Radoslav starts explaining that idea)

    → Reply

  • »

    teraqqq
    11 months ago, show (+5) #
    »
    »
    teraqqq
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    -9

    Translation error. For the analysis, it was easier for me to introduce the concepts of linear algebra. And I tried to calm people who are not familiar with this kind of reasoning.

    So that people would be more motivated to read the analysis and learn why Gauss works in this problem.

    Using Gauss in this kind of problem, instead of searching for an invariant, seemed like an interesting idea to me. This task has a clear problem that you can just write a solution without proof, but it seems that a person who is not able to provide the proof above will not do this.

    → Reply

      _istil

      »
      »
      _istil
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      -15

      I think the XOR Basis algorithm (without linear algebra explanations) is already much more well-known (and easier to get familiar with) than a linear algebra concept. And I don't think the problem is hard enough for the position in either perspective.

        Could you explain XOR Basis without referring to the concepts of linear algebra?

          »

          Dominater069
          11 months ago, show #

        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          _istil
          11 months ago, show #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          _istil
          11 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          Let me be more specific: to someone who has studied linear algebra systematically, XOR Basis isn't an issue (It should just be trivial). To someone who hasn't, it makes no difference if you just make a name change in the terms and be less professional in introducing this algorithm, which does make the concept more acceptable.

          I know the essence of the algorithm is just linear algebra (and knowing it makes mastering the algorithm fast), but it does not mean linear algebra is a preknowledge here. The point is, the name of a term in linear algebra does not help reduce the problem itself (and even makes the editorial writer more tiring since he needs to type large LaTeX formulas). On the assumption that the editorial is written for those who skipped linear algebra classes / hasn't ever com across linear algebra, I think the current tutorial is a failure: like the solution steps are actually A to B to C all the way to Z, he explained X to Y to Z carefully, but what about those who only get to know A?

          I should have realized that there are many differences in Chinese and Western education, especially in fields like advanced Maths. Thanks to linear algebra being included in many important exams, the Internet is full of low-quality, incomprehensible tutorials of linear algebra in Chinese. On the other hand, I've seen some nice blogs/articles on XOR Basis without referring to any of linear algebra, such as the thesis of Chen Xinyang, in the Collection of Theses of the 2025 Chinese National Trining Team.

          → Reply

      bestial-42-centroids
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
      ← Rev. 3 →  
      +8

      I think there are better ways to motivate the fact that you divide n by the largest power of two possible than "I do it, here is a matrix, look I can swap rows and do transvections thus I can use gaussian...". Typically, you could have explained things with a geometrical point of view instead (so talking about bit vectors, explaining how you end up with n2kn2k vectors, and how the problem reduces to comparing the span of two set of vectors (which you can do with xor basis, or gaussian elimination)).

      → Reply

    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +3

    so in this case, you may delete up to 3 bars, and any house that can be a median after up to 3 deletions is valid for a house location.

    deleting 10 and 100, you have 1 and 1000 left, and any house between them achieves the minimum score of absolute distance to bars = 999. Therefore all of these houses are valid in this case, so you have 1000 possible houses.

    → Reply

For D1B, I don't understand why the answer is 22 for non-degenerate cycle case. Such a component will have equal number of edges and vertices, where choosing all the vertices is the only valid assignment.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    YuukiS
    11 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    YuukiS
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0

    You can view the task as assigning a direction to each edge such that every vertex has in-degree at most 1. For the case of a non-degenerate cycle, flipping all of the edges in the cycle will give you another valid assignment (in the original problem, even if the set of selected cells is the same, there are two possible ways to assign them to p2,p4,...,p2kp2,p4,...,p2k).

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      synthborne
      11 months ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      synthborne
      11 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Understood, thanks!

      → Reply
  • »
    »
    Schmoov
    11 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Schmoov
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Making one decision at any vertex of the cycles forces your decision for all others. You basically choose in which direction the cycle goes.

    → Reply

+32

Div.1 E actually boils down to this ucup problem. The last part of this problem is weaker than the ucup problem, so you don't have to do all the data structure problem solving by yourself.

However, since it's only the last part of the solution while the whole analysis is original, I guess it's still OK. Though maybe this sort of problem is too well-known and is trivial to many participants without seeing that ucup problem.

Can someone please explain me problem div2 B on this test case please? 3 1 6 7 9

→ Reply
  • »

    _D-One_
    11 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    _D-One_
    11 months ago, hide # ^ |
    ← Rev. 2 →  
    0 
    You can remove **7**

    Now you have 6 , 9

    For all **6, 7, 8, 9**; |x-y| will be the same. 
    6 = > |6-9| = 3 
    7 => |6-7| + |9-7| = 3
    8 => |8-6| + |8-9| = 3
    9 => |6-9| = 3

    Sad, was not able to solve it in the contest. :')

jeroenodb
11 months ago, show (+3) #
»
jeroenodb
11 months ago, hide # |
 
+40

For F there's a conceptually easier solution with the same complexity using broken profile DP. The main idea is that you make a dp over rows, and then over columns. You decide the state of each grid cell one by one, and you need to make sure you know the state of some grid cells that influence the grid cell you are currently deciding. It turns out you can use a bitmask of the following form:

0..12345
6789X...

So a rolling bitmask of n+1n+1 bits and the first bit of the previous row. So you have ≤2n+2≤2n+2 states. You can easily see you can recalculate this bitmask when the XX cell gets shifted, and you always have access to the bits j−1j−1,j+1j+1 and jj modnmodn, to transition with the right costs added.

I think this and the easier copy-paste solution to E make the round really unbalanced, as the hard end has a lot of problems which have alternate much easier solutions. And for C and D the hard part can also be copy-pasted / is just a trick many have seen before.

→ Reply

    I am having a hard time trying to prove that the only constraints on the flows at any stage are on the sum of subsets. Does anyone have some material I can read up?

phongnc
11 months ago, show #
»
phongnc
11 months ago, hide # |
 
0

In 2B, there's another way to prove why f(x)f(x) is minimal at the median. We know that |t|+|u|=|t|+|−u|≥|t−u||t|+|u|=|t|+|−u|≥|t−u|, and equality happens iff tu≤0tu≤0.

Let's say we have n=2mn=2m open bars at locations a1≤a2≤⋯≤a2ma1≤a2≤⋯≤a2m. The case where nn is odd is similar.

Then,

f(x)=|x−a1|+|x−a2|+⋯+|x−am|+|x−am+1|+⋯+|x−a2m−1|+|x−a2m|=(|x−a1|+|x−a2m|)+(|x−a2|+|x−a2m−1|)+⋯+(|x−am|+|x−am+1|)≥|a2m−a1|+|a2m−1−a2|+⋯+|am+1−am|(a constant)f(x)=|x−a1|+|x−a2|+⋯+|x−am|+|x−am+1|+⋯+|x−a2m−1|+|x−a2m|=(|x−a1|+|x−a2m|)+(|x−a2|+|x−a2m−1|)+⋯+(|x−am|+|x−am+1|)≥|a2m−a1|+|a2m−1−a2|+⋯+|am+1−am|(a constant)

Equality is achieved iff

⎧⎩⎨⎪⎪⎪⎪⎪⎪a1≤x≤a2ma2≤x≤a2m−1⋮am≤x≤am+1,{a1≤x≤a2ma2≤x≤a2m−1⋮am≤x≤am+1,

which is equivalent to the final range am≤x≤am+1am≤x≤am+1 (a range contains all the ranges below it; that's also why we rearrange the terms earlier: to ensure equality is actually achievable). In the case where n=2m+1n=2m+1, the final range is reduced to x=am+1x=am+1.

→ Reply

Div2E/Div1C:

we use the Chinese remainder theorem:
vxt+x≡0(modn)
vyt+y≡0(modn)

Can somebody please explain how to come from this to what the original CRT solves:

x ≡ a1 (mod n1)
x ≡ a2 (mod n2)

Ok, t -> x, n -> n1 = n2, n — x -> a1, n — y -> a2. But what to do with vx and vy?

    LuckyAgrawal
    9 months ago, show (+1) #
    »

    LuckyAgrawal
    9 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    In the For loop condition while checking for the same , greater than by 1 or more part, when the current element is greater than previous element by more than 1 , we should also check the cnt4 of the current element. If we don't we might skip it and move to the next element.

    Your code 

       for(int i=1;i<n;++i){
        if(a[i]==a[i-1])continue;
        if(a[i]-a[i-1]==1){
            if(freq[a[i]]>=2)cnt2++;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=4)cnt4++;
            if(cnt2>1 || cnt4){
                yes
                return;
            }
        }
        else{
            cnt2=0,cnt4=0;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=2)cnt2++;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=4)cnt4++;
        }
    }

    Modification 

        for(int i=1;i<n;++i){
        if(a[i]==a[i-1])continue;
        if(a[i]-a[i-1]==1){
            if(freq[a[i]]>=2)cnt2++;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=4)cnt4++;
            if(cnt2>1 || cnt4){
                yes
                return;
            }
        }
        else{
            cnt2=0,cnt4=0;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=2)cnt2++;
            if(freq[a[i]]>=4)cnt4++;
            if(cnt4){
                  yes
                  return;
            }
        }
    }
    → Reply

How to prove the O(nlogn) solution for Div1.E?

Although the segment-spilt part is easily to be proved O(nlogn),but the segment-merge?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Noobish_Monk
    10 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    Noobish_Monk
    10 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +14

    These segment trees work in O(n log n)O(n log n) amortized. The potential is the total number of nodes that trees have.

    Then segment-split creates no more that log nlog n additional nodes.

    Segment-merge works in O(common vertices)O(common vertices) and deletes that exact amount of nodes.

    → Reply
»

Xellos
10 months ago, show #

Xellos
10 months ago, hide # |
 
0

319833751 sqrt solution for E. Not even all that squeezed. For small DD, I do DP on sqrt-sized blocks "if a prefix was covered, how much more to cover the whole segment and what prefix of the next segment will it cover" and compute greedy covering quickly over blocks. For large DD, I simulate greedy covering directly using info "find next active element or say it's more than sqrt further away".

→ Reply

8 months ago, hide # |
 
0

[Sorry for necroposting]

Alternative solution to Div1F without using DP. Construct the flow network only for the first day. Run Dinic to find flow from ss to t1t1.

Add edges from day 11 to day 22. Run Dinic to find flow from t1t1 to t2t2.

Add edges from day 22 to day 33. Run Dinic to find flow from t2t2 to t3t3.

Add edges from day 33 to day 44. Run Dinic to find flow from t3t3 to t4t4.

...

Very close to TL, but fits without any DP and is easy to code. 328939831 (copy-paste Dinic from a template)

→ Reply

In 2097F - Lost Luggage, you can fit Highest-Label-Preflow-Push into the time limit (if you don't recompute the flow from scratch in each iteration, but instead just reroute the excess from the old sink to the new sink). 334089270

Tadas
2 months ago, show #

Tadas
2 months ago, hide # |
 
0

I think there's a typo in div2C: s_{x+1}...s_{y+1}s_{y+2} — the seating arrangements from day x to day y — this is fine, but later it follows "If s_{x+1} != 0 ... then Vadim can definitively convince at least one student on day x or day y" Shouldn't it be s_{x+2} != 0 as predictions on x day are (s_{x+1}, s_{x+2})={(0, 1), (1, 1)}?

Also in statement "which is the only case where Vadim has no winning strategy according to the previous point, when n <= 3" I agree that there's no winning strategy in n<=3 case with this configuration, but when n>=4, it boils down to the winning strategy as from the 1st bullet point, a_i+1<=a_{i+1} (no breaks argument).

→ Reply
